title: The trigger of someone touching your belongings without permission

htmlContent:
Your things are an extension of your body

I remember the exact feeling. I came home from work and found that someone had moved my coffee mug from its usual spot on the kitchen counter. It was just a mug. A ceramic thing I bought at a thrift store for three dollars. But my chest tightened. My jaw clenched. My hands started shaking. And I felt this surge of rage that made no logical sense at all.

Here's the thing. That reaction wasn't about the mug. It was about my nervous system interpreting a boundary violation as a physical threat. And if you've ever felt that same visceral response when someone touches your stuff without asking, you're not crazy. You're not overreacting. You're having a trauma response.

Peter Levine, the founder of Somatic Experiencing, talks about how our bodies store memories of boundary violations. When someone touches your belongings without permission, your nervous system doesn't distinguish between a physical boundary and an object boundary. To your survival brain, they're the same thing. Someone crossed a line. Someone entered your space without consent. And your body remembers what happened the last time someone did that.

Think about it. If you grew up in an environment where your privacy was routinely violated, where your room was searched, your diary read, your things taken or moved without explanation, then your nervous system learned something crucial. It learned that boundaries are not safe. It learned that your space can be invaded at any moment. And it learned to stay hypervigilant, always scanning for the next violation.

That hypervigilance doesn't just disappear when you move out of that environment. It lives in your body. It lives in the way your shoulders tense when someone reaches toward your desk. It lives in the way your stomach drops when you notice your phone has been moved. It lives in the flash of anger that feels completely disproportionate to the situation.

One resource that often resonates deeply with C-PTSD survivors is Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link). Pete Walker explains how emotional flashbacks can be triggered by seemingly small events, including boundary violations around personal space and belongings. He breaks down why these reactions feel so intense and how to start working with them instead of against them.

The nervous system's logic is not your logic

Here's where it gets complicated. Your logical brain knows that someone borrowing your book without asking is not a life-or-death situation. But your nervous system doesn't operate on logic. It operates on pattern recognition. And when it detects a pattern that matches a past violation, it activates the same survival responses it used back then.

Stephen Porges, the researcher who developed Polyvagal Theory, explains this through the concept of neuroception. Neuroception is your nervous system's ability to scan for safety and danger without your conscious awareness. It's happening all the time, beneath the surface of your thoughts. And it's incredibly fast. Faster than your thinking brain can catch up.

So when someone touches your belongings without permission, your neuroception might detect a threat. Your body might drop into fight mode, with that surge of anger and tension. Or it might drop into freeze mode, where you feel numb and disconnected. Or it might drop into fawn mode, where you smile and pretend it's fine while your insides are screaming.

None of these responses are wrong. They're all valid survival strategies that kept you safe at some point. The problem is that they don't match the current situation. And that mismatch creates confusion, shame, and self-judgment. You end up thinking things like "Why am I so upset about this? It's just a thing. I'm being ridiculous."

But you're not being ridiculous. You're being human. You're having a nervous system response that makes perfect sense given your history. And the first step toward healing is recognizing that this response is not a sign of brokenness. It's a sign that your survival system is working exactly the way it was designed to work.

A book that many trauma survivors find genuinely helpful is The Body Keeps the Score (paid link). Bessel van der Kolk walks through how trauma is stored in the body and how everyday triggers can activate those stored memories. Reading it helped me understand why my body reacts the way it does, and it might help you too.

The shame cycle and the path out of it

Here's the part that nobody talks about enough. After the initial reaction fades, the shame sets in. You replay the moment in your head. You tell yourself you should have been calmer. You should have been more reasonable. You should have just let it go. And then you feel even worse than you did before.

This shame cycle is deeply embedded in trauma responses. Janina Fisher, a trauma specialist who integrates Internal Family Systems (IFS) with trauma treatment, talks about how our inner critic often takes over after a trigger. The inner critic tells us we're too sensitive, too dramatic, too broken. It tries to protect us from future shame by shaming us first.

But here's what I've learned. The shame cycle only deepens when we try to fight it. When we tell ourselves we shouldn't feel what we feel, we create an internal war. Part of us is activated and scared. Another part is judging that activation. And those two parts are locked in conflict, generating more stress, more tension, and more dysregulation.

The path out of this cycle is not to get rid of the reaction. The path is to make space for it. To notice what's happening in your body without trying to change it. To say to yourself, "Okay, my nervous system is activated right now. That makes sense. I've been through things that taught my body to be on high alert. I don't have to fix this. I just have to be with it."

This is not easy. It takes practice. It takes patience. And it takes a willingness to sit with discomfort instead of running from it. But over time, this approach starts to shift the nervous system's patterns. The reactions don't disappear completely, but they become less intense. They become shorter. They become something you can observe rather than something that consumes you.

A structured workbook that many C-PTSD survivors find practical is The Complex PTSD Workbook (paid link). It offers concrete exercises for working with triggers, including boundary-related ones, and helps you build skills for regulating your nervous system over time.

Practical ways to work with this trigger

So what do you do when someone touches your stuff and your body goes into full alarm mode? Here are some things that have helped me and the people I work with.

First, ground yourself in the present moment. Feel your feet on the floor. Feel the weight of your body in the chair. Look around the room and name three things you can see. This is not about ignoring your feelings. It's about giving your nervous system information that you are here, now, in this moment, and that the past is not happening right now.

Second, name what's happening without judgment. You can say to yourself, "I'm having a trauma response. My nervous system thinks I'm in danger. But I'm actually safe right now. This feeling is from the past." This kind of naming activates the prefrontal cortex, which can help calm the amygdala's alarm signals.

Third, use physical regulation tools. A weighted blanket can provide deep pressure stimulation that helps calm the nervous system. A weighted blanket that many find calming for their nervous system is YnM Weighted Blanket 15 lbs (paid link). The weight creates a sense of containment and safety that can be incredibly grounding during a trigger.

Fourth, set boundaries after you've regulated, not during the trigger. Trying to set a boundary while you're in fight-or-flight mode often backfires. You might come across as aggressive or overly emotional, which can create more conflict. Wait until your nervous system has calmed down. Then you can say something like, "I need my things to stay where I left them. Can you please ask before touching them?"

Fifth, give yourself permission to have this trigger. It's not a flaw. It's not something to be fixed. It's a signal from your body that something important was violated, and your body is trying to protect you. Honor that signal. Thank your body for trying to keep you safe. And then gently remind yourself that you're not in that old situation anymore.

The messy reality of healing

I want to be honest with you about something. Healing from this trigger does not mean you'll never feel that surge of rage again. It doesn't mean you'll become someone who doesn't care when their boundaries are crossed. And it definitely doesn't mean you'll become some zen master who floats through life untouched by other people's carelessness.

What healing looks like is more subtle. It looks like noticing the reaction sooner. It looks like recovering faster. It looks like having less shame about the reaction. It looks like being able to say, "Hey, that bothered me" without feeling like you're making a huge deal out of nothing.

It looks like understanding that this trigger is not a sign of weakness. It's a sign of wisdom. Your body learned something important about safety and boundaries, and it's trying to protect you based on that learning. The problem is not the learning itself. The problem is that the learning is outdated. Your body is still operating from a time when you had no control over your environment. And now you do.

That doesn't make the trigger go away overnight. But it does change your relationship to it. Instead of fighting against your body's response, you can work with it. You can say, "I see you. I hear you. You're trying to keep me safe. And I've got this now."

Sit with that. Let it land. Because this is the real work of healing. Not erasing the past, but building a new relationship with the parts of you that were shaped by it. Not becoming perfect, but becoming more compassionate with yourself in the middle of your imperfection.

And if someone touches your stuff tomorrow and you feel that familiar surge of anger, I hope you remember this. You're not broken. You're not overreacting. You're a person with a history, and your body is doing exactly what it learned to do to survive. That's not a failure. That's your resilience.
